project.title: Evaluation of Ca37 monoclonal antibody against pathogenic fungal Candidozyma auris
project.projectDescription: Candidozyma auris, previously known as Candida auris, is a recently found pathogenic yeast that causes systemic infections, showing a high mortality rate. Moreover, this species is highly resistant to the commonly used antifungal drugs and some of the strains are multiresistant. This fungus is also able to cause outbreaks in hospital settings. All of this has cause an alarm in the health care system. Therefore, finding alternative treatments for C. auris is critical. In this sense, our research group developed a monoclonal antibody (Ca37) against Candida albicans alcohol dehydrogenase enzyme (Adh) that successfully reduced the growth of the fungus in vitro and also showed a protective effect in vivo. Due to the high homology between both fungal alcohol dehydrogenases, we wanted to test the effect of Ca37 over C. auris. In order to do this, first we assessed if the monoclonal antibody also recognized C. auris Adh by sequencing the spots detected by two dimensional western blot when using the monoclonal antibody as first antibody. Two spots were detected in this western blot. The sequencing showed a mixture of proteins, but in both cases, Adh was one of the identified proteins.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: The cell wall proteome of C. auris CECT13225 was obtained following the method published by Pitartch and coworkers (2002). Briefly C. auris was grown at 37 °C and 120 rpms O/N. The obtained pellet was then washed five times with ice-cold lysis buffer (10 mM tris-HCL, pH 7.4 and 1 mM phenylmethylsulfonyl fluoride (PMSF)). Cells were resuspended in the same buffer and added to eppendorfs containing the same volume of crystal beads (0.5 mm diameter) and disrupted at 28 Hz for 20 min using the Millmix 20 Bead-Beater (Thetnica, Eslovenia, Europe). Sample was then centrifuge for 5 min at 14,000 g. The pellet containing the cell walls was washed five times with each of the following ice-cold solutions: distilled water, 5% NaCl, 2% NaCl, 1% NaCl and 1 mM PMSF. Then, cell walls were resuspended in sodium dodecyl sulphate (SDS) extraction buffer (50 mM tris-HCl, pH 8, 0.1 M EDTA, 2% (w/v) SDS and 10 mM dithiothreitol (DTT) and boiled at 100 °C for 10 min. Samples were centrifuged for 5 min at 14,000 g to recover the cell wall-associated proteins.  These proteins were precipitated by adding four volumes of 10% trichloroacetic acid and 0.07% 2-mercaptoethanol in acetone, and the obtained protein pellet was dissolved on rehydration buffer (7 M Urea, 2 M thiourea, %4 (w/v) CHAPS, 20 mM Tris). Then, they were separated by two-dimensional electrophoresis. For that 285 µg of protein were loaded into 7 cm long Immobiline DryStrip gels (pH 3-10, GE Healthcare) for the isoelectric focusing (IEF). The DryStrips were left to rehydrate overnight, then followed by isoelectric focusing at 500 V for 2000 Vhr, 1000 V for 3000 Vhr, 5000 V for 10 000 Vhr, and 5000 V for 40 000 Vhr; 50 μA per strip. Next, proteins were reduced 15 min equilibration buffer (6 M urea, 75 mM Tris-HCl pH 6.8, 25.5% [v/v] glycerol, 2% [w/v] SDS, 0.002% [w/v] bromophenol blue) with 1% (w/v) DTT and then alkylated for 15 min the same equilibration buffer with 2.5% (w/v) iodoacetamide. The strips were run in 10% acrylamide gels in the PROTEAN II xl Cell (Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA) at 70 mA per gel. The gels were stained with Coomassie Brilliant Blue, or transferred to a membrane to perform a Western Blot.  In the second case, proteins were transferred to Amersham Hybond-P PVDF membranes (GE Healthcare). First the membrane were immersed in methanol, distilled water, and transfer buffer (5,82 g/L tris-base, 2.93 g/L glycine and 200 ml methanol, pH 9.2) for 1 h at 400 mA. After immersing the membrane in methanol, distilled water and Tris buffered saline (TBS, Tris-HCl pH 7.5 amd 150 mM NaCl), it was blocked for 2 h with TBS Milk (TBSM, TBS supplemented with 5% (w/v) low fat dried milk and 0,1% (v/v) Tween 20). Then, it was washed 5 times with TBS for 5 minutes. Ca37 monoclonal antibody at 60 ug/mL was added and incubated O/N at 4 °C. After washing the membrane 5 times with TBS, 5 minutes each, anti-mouse IgG-HRP conjugated secondary antibody (DC02L, Merck) was added diluted 1:100,000 in TBSM and incubated for 30 minutes. The membrane was washed as before with TBS and the detection was visualized using the ECL Prime chemiluminiscence kit (NZYTech, Lisbon, Portugal) in a G:BOX Chemi imaging system (Syngene, Cambridge, United Kingdom). All incubation steps were performed at room temperature and agitation unless otherwise specified. The spots that were recognized by the monoclonal antibody were manually extracted from the gel stained with Coomassie Brilliant Blue G250. At the general services of research of the institution (SGIker), the gel extracts were swollen in digestion buffer (50 mM NH4HCO3, 12.5 ng/µL trypsin (Roche, Basel, Switzerland)) and incubated at 37 °C overnight. Then peptides were extracted, first with 25 mM NH4HCO3 and ACN, and second with 0.1% (v/v) trifluoroacetic acid and ACN. The recovered supernatants were pooled and dried in a SpeedVac (Thermo Electron, Waltham, MA, USA) and then dissolved in 10 µL of 0.1% (v/v) formic acid (FA) and sonicated for 5 min. The liquid chromatography was carried out on a EASY nLC-1200 (Thermo) (Waters, Milford, MA, USA) and the mass spectrophotometry in a Exploris 240 (Thermo). To do this, 8 µL from each sample was loaded into a Symmetry 300 C18, 180  µm × 20 mm precolumn (Waters) and washed with 0.1% (v/v) FA at a flow rate of 5 µL/min for 3 min. The precolumn was connected to a BEH130 C18, 75 µm × 200 mm, 1.7 µm (Waters), equilibrated in 3% (v/v) ACN and 0.1% (v/v) FA. Peptides were eluted with a 30 min linear gradient of 3–60% (v/v) ACN directly onto a NanoEase Emitter (Waters). Capillary voltage was set to 3500 V and data-dependent MS/MS acquisitions performed on precursors with charge states of 2, 3, or 4 over a survey m/z range of 350–1990. Reference: Pitarch, A., Sanchez, M., Nombela, C. and Gil, C., 2002. Sequential Fractionation and Two-dimensional Gel Analysis Unravels the Complexity of the Dimorphic Fungus Candida albicans Cell Wall Proteome. Mollecular & Cellular Proteomics. 1.12: 967-982
project.dataProcessingProtocol: The data processing was done using Proteome Discoverer 2.2 (Thermo), and the search for protein identification of the sequenced peptides was performed in the Uniprot database (https://www.uniprot.org/), restricted to C. auris. More than one result was obtained for the spots; in both of then Adh was present with a coverage percentage higher than 40 and a score above 45%.

Evaluation of the Ca37 Monoclonal Antibody Targeting Alcohol Dehydrogenase Against <i>Candidozyma auris</i> (<i>Candida auris</i>) In Vitro and In Vivo. J Fungi (Basel). 2025 11(12):864
